name: Allego
alexConclusions:
Sole focus on public charging, primarily destination. 35k stations. Probably around 2-3k DC and the rest is AC. They already have covered a lot of the needs for a public CPO. The question is, at that scale, how much do they struggle with operations — which is something a solid CPMS can help with, or totally ruin.

Also, their recent CEO change indicates a push for profitability, so I am not expecting aggressive expansion plans yet, but maybe in the following years, part of their strategy could be M&A, especially given that this guys has experience with.

Now, another scenario would be that they sell to PE, but I am doubtful. Most likely they will first optimise cost, and then think about which companies are meaningful for M&A expansion.

Typically, at this stage they should have figured cost reductions, operations at scale, profitability etc., but given the new CEO, that will most likely be the first big stage or milestone. The next would be sell or expand.

Definitely worth pursuing, unless they have already started talking/evaluating other CPMSes or they are completely closed off to the idea.

salesSignals.text: CEO change — Feb 2025. Steven Salo hired specifically to drive profitability. Strong signal for cost optimisation, procurement reviews, and openness to operational tooling that reduces OPEX at scale.

Scale without confirmed profitability. 35k stations is a large footprint. Operational complexity compounds fast at this size — uptime, billing, roaming settlement, and scheduling are all likely pain points.
Active Dynamics F&O billing automation hiring. They are actively building backend complexity to automate billing for 35k+ charge points — potential gap where a better CPMS integration could help.
M&A profile emerging post-profitability. New CEO has M&A experience. Likely playbook: stabilise → optimise → acquire. A scalable CPMS becomes critical if they plan to onboard acquired networks.
Multi-country complexity. 18 countries with different regulations, roaming structures, and local partnerships. Managing this at scale across a single CPMS is non-trivial.

openQuestions: Are they currently in active CPMS evaluation conversations with other vendors?
What CPMS are they running today — proprietary in-house, or a third-party platform?
Is the new CEO mandating a full tech stack review, or primarily finance and operations?
What are the actual pain points at scale — uptime, billing accuracy, roaming settlement, driver app quality?
Has ChargeTrip been confirmed as the route planning provider, or is it still an assumption from app observation?

synthesis.verdictText: Large-scale CPO with a confirmed public network, active backend investment, and a new leadership mandate for profitability. The combination of scale + cost pressure + potential M&A ambition makes this a high-value prospect — unless CPMS conversations are already too advanced elsewhere.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 1 — Cost reduction is the first priority
hypotheses.text: New CEO Steven Salo was hired to make the business profitable. The most likely first move is operational cost reduction, not aggressive expansion. Any tool that can demonstrably reduce OPEX per charge point (fewer support tickets, better uptime, billing accuracy) will have a strong ROI story right now.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 2 — M&A expansion follows profitability
hypotheses.text: Salo has M&A experience. The likely playbook: stabilise → optimise → acquire smaller CPOs or adjacent players. A scalable CPMS becomes critical if they plan to onboard acquired networks efficiently — a proprietary in-house system becomes a bottleneck at that point.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 3 — AC destination is the operationally underserved segment
hypotheses.text: DC fast charging is well-covered commercially. The bulk of their 35k stations is likely AC at destination sites. This segment has distinct CPMS requirements — longer dwell times, revenue share settlement, and site host reporting — worth validating as potential gaps.

uncertainties: Current CPMS is unknown. If proprietary and in-house, the sales motion is displacement — harder. If third-party with poor fit, it's a switch story with a clearer path.
No confirmed PE exit signals. A sale to PE would likely pause procurement decisions significantly.
ChargeTrip as route planning provider is an assumption from app observation, not confirmed from official sources.

contacts.name: Steven Salo
contacts.title: CEO
contacts.note: Hired Feb 2025. Primary strategic decision-maker with a profitability mandate and M&A background.

synthesis.contactsNote: Secondary targets: CTO / Head of Operations / Head of Technology — whoever owns the CPMS relationship. Identify via LinkedIn. Look for anyone with "charging platform", "backend", or "infrastructure" in their title.
